_comment: 40 maturity questions, 5 per dimension. Each question maps answer options to DAMA-DMBOK maturity levels 1-5. See content/schemas/questions.schema.json for the full schema.

questions.question: Is there a named person accountable for each major data domain in your organisation?
questions.help_text: A data domain is a distinct subject area of data — for example, customer data, financial data, HR data, or operational data.

options.label: No — nobody is formally responsible for any data domain. Ownership is unclear.
Informally — a few people are known to look after certain data, but it is not documented or recognised as a role.
Partially — named owners exist for our most important data domains, with documented responsibilities.
Broadly — most domains have named owners within a defined accountability framework, with regular check-ins.
Fully — every domain has a named owner with active stewardship duties, regular reviews, and clear escalation paths.

questions.question: Does your organisation have documented data policies covering access, quality, retention, and acceptable use?
questions.help_text: Policies don't need to be a single document — separate policies for each topic are fine. The question is whether they exist and people know where to find them.

options.label: No documented policies exist. Rules are communicated informally if at all.
Some policies exist (often written for a compliance deadline) but they are hard to find and not widely read.
Core policies are documented, accessible, and applied consistently across our main systems and teams.
Policies are comprehensive, regularly reviewed on a set schedule, and ownership of each policy is clearly assigned.
Policies are version-controlled, enforced through tooling where possible, and updated based on incidents and regulatory changes.

questions.question: Does your organisation have a data governance body — such as a committee, council, or working group — with real decision-making authority?
questions.help_text: This could be a formal Data Governance Board or an informal cross-functional group. The key question is whether it can make and enforce decisions about data.

options.label: No governance body exists. Data decisions are made ad hoc by whoever is available.
There are occasional cross-team meetings about data, but no standing forum with a defined remit.
A governance body exists with a defined remit, meets regularly, and has resolved at least some real data disputes.
The governance body has documented authority, a published meeting schedule, minutes, and an active backlog of decisions.
The governance body drives strategic data direction, reviews metrics, owns the policy framework, and reports to senior leadership.

questions.question: Does your organisation classify data by sensitivity — for example, distinguishing between public, internal, confidential, and sensitive personal data?
questions.help_text: A classification scheme means data assets are labelled or tagged with their sensitivity level, not just that the categories exist in a policy document.

options.label: No classification scheme. All data is treated the same regardless of sensitivity.
Sensitivity categories exist on paper but data is not consistently labelled or tagged in practice.
A classification scheme is in place and applied consistently to our most sensitive and highest-risk data assets.
Classification is applied broadly across the estate and is integrated with access control — permissions reflect classification level.
Classification is automated at ingestion, enforced by tooling, and linked to retention, masking, and audit policies.

questions.question: How does your organisation measure and track compliance with its data governance policies?
questions.help_text: This is about whether you know whether policies are being followed — not just whether the policies exist.

options.label: We do not measure compliance. Policy adherence depends on individuals being aware of and choosing to follow the rules.
Compliance is checked occasionally, usually before an audit or in response to an incident.
Key compliance indicators are tracked and reported at least quarterly, with a process for raising and resolving breaches.
Compliance metrics are part of regular governance reporting. Non-compliance triggers a formal triage and resolution process.
Policy compliance is continuously monitored through automated tooling. Exceptions are detected and escalated in near real-time.
